title: Exposure Bias: The Training Gap
concept: Exposure bias occurs because the model is exposed to perfect ground-truth inputs during training, but must rely on its own noisy predictions at inference.

exercises.prompt: During standard text generation training (Teacher Forcing), what inputs are fed to the model at each step?

options.text: Randomly selected words from a dictionary

exercises.explain: During training, we use 'Teacher Forcing' to feed the correct next word (ground-truth) into the model at each step, rather than letting it guess and drift off course.

cards.title: The Slippery Slope of AI Text
cards.body: Have you ever noticed an AI writer start a sentence perfectly, but slowly drift into absolute nonsense by the end? This sudden crash is often caused by a hidden trap called "Exposure Bias." Let's find out why it happens!

cards.title: What is Exposure Bias?
cards.body: Exposure bias is a training-testing mismatch. During training, a model gets the correct 'ground-truth' words at every step (Teacher Forcing). But during testing, it must generate text step-by-step using its own previous predictions. If it makes one small mistake early on, it has no idea how to recover, leading to a cascade of errors!

cards.title: The Training Wheel Wobble
cards.body: Imagine learning to ride a bicycle while training wheels are always attached. During training, you never fall because the wheels hold you up. But on test day, the training wheels are taken away! Suddenly, a tiny wobble makes you lose balance completely because you never practiced recovering from your own mistakes.

cards.title: A Translation Slip-Up
cards.body:
When translating 'I love cats' to French:
• Training: The model predicts 'J'adore', then we immediately feed it the correct word 'les' even if it got it wrong.
• Inference: The model predicts 'J'adore', then guesses 'des' instead of 'les'. Because it has to use its own guess, it gets confused and outputs gibberish: 'J'adore des chapeaux bananes...'

cards.title: Myth: Perfect Training Makes Perfect Output
cards.body: We might think that training a model with 100% perfect, clean data guarantees it will output perfect text. But in reality, if the model only sees perfection during training, it becomes fragile and panics at the first tiny mistake it makes in the real world.

cards.title: Training vs. Testing
cards.body: Compare how the model processes text during training versus how it generates text in the real world (inference).

right.text: Input is the model's own previous prediction. Mistakes accumulate!

left.text: Input is always the correct ground-truth word, keeping the model on track.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary cause of exposure bias in text generation models?

options.text: The model is exposed to too many biased internet articles.
The model gets perfect inputs during training but must use its own imperfect outputs during testing.
The model forgets its vocabulary because the dataset is too large.
The computer running the model overheats during long text generation.

exercises.explain: During training, Teacher Forcing provides the correct ground-truth word at each step. This creates a gap because the model never learns how to handle its own mistakes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using 'Teacher Forcing' during training is the main reason exposure bias occurs.
exercises.explain: True! Teacher forcing feeds the model correct answers during training, preventing it from experiencing and recovering from its own mistakes until it is too late (during inference).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phase name to complete the explanation of the training-to-testing gap.
exercises.sentence: While the model sees perfect data during training, it must rely on its own noisy predictions during inference.
exercises.explain: During training, the model receives perfect ground-truth inputs. However, during inference (testing/real-world generation), it must rely on its own previous, sometimes noisy, predictions.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following AI outputs shows a classic sign of exposure bias, where a small early error causes the rest of the text to completely unravel?

options.text: \"I want to go to the parkk today.\"
\"Once upon a time, there was a dog who lived in a house of cheese and blue sky running running fast blue water.\"

exercises.explain: Option B shows cumulative error drift. Option A is a simple spelling error, and Option C is just a repetitive output, neither representing the classic snowballing failure of exposure bias.

exercises.prompt: You want to redesign your training pipeline to reduce exposure bias. Which of these strategies would help close the training-to-inference gap by exposing the model to its own potential mistakes?

options.text: Feeding the model its own past predictions during training instead of ground-truth tokens
Adding random noise to the model's vocabulary list
Only training the model on positive movie reviews
Using a smaller dataset for training than for testing

exercises.explain: Exposure bias is caused by teacher forcing, where we feed perfect target data (ground-truth) during training. This prevents the model from learning how to recover from its own cumulative errors when generating text on its own (inference).
